sentences.text: I don't remember that there was any great jubilation or celebration.

[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Somehow, we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] stayed together for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] another night and then in the morning ventured out beyond the confines of this little camp and walked through the woods to the main road.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The main road, which turned out to be the main highway from Vienna, [? road ?] number one, was extremely busy.
There was a tall American soldier with a rifle, and we witnessed a motorcycle with a sidecar arriving with a German officer in the sidecar.

And the [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] American soldier asked for his gun but otherwise let the motorcycle continue on its journey, which we were extremely surprised that the Americans would let them pass rather than shoot them or intern them.

We crossed the road and were somehow following the tattered crowd, dragging ourselves to the nearest village, which was the village of Gunskirchen, and ended up near the railway station, where there was a huge storage shed or storage house that was the iron reserve, food reserve of the German army.
And the Allies, the Americans have opened it so that everybody could go in and take what they wanted.
They were almost intermingled with us.
The scene, the confusion-- and they would come to this food storage house with carts and horses and shovel the sugar and load bags of flour or whatever raw food material there was and take it away.
And the Americans didn't interfere with that.
And we [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] also took some food and [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] proceeded to make a fire there and try and cook something.
I don't remember exactly what.
But I think we were keenly aware that how dangerous it was to, to eat anything we, we put our hands on.
And indeed, many, many people died within the next hours because their system just couldn't take any food, any normal food.
None, none of us, as to the best of my knowledge, succumbed to that.
I should also say that as we were walking to this village, we saw a profusion of American troops.
And what stands out vividly in my mind, if anything is vivid at all, is a long row of huge tanks with black troops sitting on top of them and throwing us bars of chocolate.
And indeed, only a few years ago, it was shown that there were, there was a black tank battalion that was-- at one point, erroneously, thought that they'd liberated Buchenwald.
And it was a big affair.
They had to actually withdraw the film.
But they were in Gunskirchen.
They didn't liberate the camp.
But they were troops that fought nearby.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We must have been quite a sight, dragging ourselves from the camp.
We spent the night near this [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] warehouse.
And the next day--
Just on the outside somewhere.
And the next day, I believe that they told us to walk towards a larger city called Wels.

And shortly after we got on the road, some Belgian troops helped us, put us on their truck, and drove us to a little place called Horsching, which was a huge Luftwaffe and German Air Force base, military base, near the town of Wels, which is, I think, the second-largest town in Austria.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were directed to pick up some mattresses and move to one of the large rooms of this, of these military barracks.
These were masonry barracks that was recently occupied by German troops.
And as we were trying to do this, I collapsed.
And my colleagues first said, well, you're being lazy.
You don't want to carry mattresses.
But they soon realized that I just couldn't get up.
And they carried me across the street to the barrack that was a hospital.
No.
And I had typhus.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] for several weeks I was just out.
I had no idea whether I was unconscious or semi-unconscious.
I have no recollection.
I know that, sort of vaguely, that everybody ate my food, that I couldn't even eat, and that they were giving me intravenous-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] feeding me intravenously.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Ever since, I just abhor needles or, or any kind of transfusions.

But bit by bit I got stronger, and many of the-- several of the supervisory American staff that supervised German orderlies who were supposed to clean for us and keep-- you know, make beds-- and the medical, American medical staff sought me out because I spoke some English when I was a child.
I took some private English lessons so I could make myself understood.
And several of those gave me their photographs and took my address and gave me theirs.
And months after I finally reached home, we kept in touch and wrote to each other.
And unfortunately, only one of them, the doctor, who was from Baltimore, kept in touch until I came here.
And we actually met once.
He died of cancer about five years ago.
He was a very special [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] surgeon, held in high esteem and took a liking to me.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was in this hospital about six weeks, very slowly regaining life while the others were mostly in another barrack on the base, kind of living as scavengers.
There were just thousands of tons of material brought in daily by huge American transport planes.

[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The Americans had everything they-- the second day of setting up camp, they had hot showers and whenever they want a bar of chocolate or a cigarette, they would open with a bayonet K-rations or whatever they were called and just take out what they want and threw the rest away.
This was in direct contrast to the Russian army that we encountered a few weeks later.

The Americans were very kind and very friendly, and the other boys who were-- some of them were also hospitalized, but most of them were simply just slowly getting back their way toward improving the situation.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] At that time there was great confusion in Europe.
And getting back home was no easy task, because there was no transport.
Trains were not running.
Bridges were down.
At one point, there was a [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] car from Czechoslovakia that came.
And it turned out it was father of one of us.
He was a physician who survived Auschwitz and by some way found out where his son was and came to take, take him home.
That was our kind of first direct contact with our home country.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] He--
It was.
It was.
Well, we were primarily thinking of getting home and hoping, mostly, that somebody from our family survived and that we will meet with them.
And I had still great hope that my mother will meet me when I get home.

I was no, in no shape to walk until-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] [SIGHS] let's say it was the first half of June [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] when I learned that a transport is-- transportation is being arranged for those who wanted to go to Czechoslovakia.
There were isolated cases where the boys simply-- either two or three would just take off and try and fend for themselves and try and get home somehow.
And they succeeded.
But those like myself who was not up to the task waited.
And in fact, I ran away from the hospital when I learned that this opportunity arose.
And I joined the remaining boys.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And we were driven to a town called Linz that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] somewhat north of Horsching.
Yes.
Because I don't think that the hospital would have released me.
Right.
Well, I'll come to it.
In Linz, which is on the Danube, the trucks took us to the harbor and put us on boats, but let's say one boat that I was in.
The Danube always had busy boat river traffic.
And there were probably very few boats left.
Anyway, the American-- there was one or two American soldiers who escorted us on this boat.
And we arrived.
We were going east now on the Danube.
And suddenly we were-- we arrived in Melk, which is a town where we have been interned for two months.
And somewhere near there, a-- oh, before we arrived to Melk, a, a Russian officer boarded the, the ship, the boat and took over the transport from the Americans.
He was an officer that looked like a vagabond with a blanket around him and dirty boots and tattered uniform.
Whereas the American soldier-- and he wasn't even an officer-- was in perfectly pressed uniform and looked impeccable.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] ship then docked in Melk.
And we were asked to leave the ship and were escorted back to the camp that we left three months earlier.
And this was a terrible shock to us because this, the memories of the conditions that prevailed there could not be easily erased.
And we were literally afraid that we were now interned by the Russians, and maybe we are no better off than we were before.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] After a day or two in this camp, we were taken by train to a town called Wiener Neustadt.
It's not Vienna.
It's a, it's a town about 50 kilometers southwest of Vienna.
We had to zigzag because the track was-- bridges were down, and the track was destroyed in many places.
And when we arrived there, the railway station was-- everything, the whole town, was in ruin, completely bombed out.
The Russian soldiers who took care of us had nothing.
They were, they were riding on these Studebaker trucks that the Americans gave the Russian army.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They had to fend for themselves for food.
They had to, to steal or loot everything.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were not supplied with anything except a, a rifle and, and ammunition and a blanket.
So it was-- that's why I say they were very different.

The population was scared of the Russians and was running into the American zone because of rapes and, and robberies and-- after all, the Russians really suffered from the Germans and Austrians terribly.
They devastated their country and killed the population.
So the Russians were not wearing white gloves.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] From Wiener Neustadt, there were no trains.
And we had to walk.
We formed groups.
And not quite sure whether we were guided here and there by a Russian soldier or how we were guided.
But it took several days to reach Slovakia, the capital of Slovakia, Bratislava.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We slept by the roadside.
We carried whatever we could, mostly stuff that the Americans gave us, some food, some soap, and just rudimentary stuff.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And my memory is of the countryside being trampled by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] horses.
The Russians were literally taking horses and cattle and chasing them towards the east.
I mean, they were just taking everything that they could.
And that sticks in my mind.
I think there were some fruit trees, that we were eating some fruit on the side of the road and that we were pretty exhausted and advanced very slowly.
When we reached the Danube, all the bridges were destroyed.
And the Americans set up a pontoon bridge.
And I recall crossing the pontoon bridge into Bratislava and being ushered into a refugee center where we were registered and sent to get a shower.
And again, everybody was afraid of contagious diseases.
So we were sent for fumigation time and again.
Local people.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] What sticks in my mind is that this was the first time we were kind of in semi-normal circumstances.
And we went to the cinema.
And we all remember seeing in Russian.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's something that we all-- sticks in our mind.
So this--
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
People were sort of feeling sorry for us.
We were no longer outcasts or, or justifiably led to slaughter.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were then-- I think the next day-- given a railway ticket to wherever we want to go.
And a few of us were going to Prague on the same train.
I recall that it was so crowded, and we had to actually sit in the, stand in the corridor.
Couldn't find a seat.
And it sticks to my mind that at a certain point, Brno, which is the capital of Moravia, two or three of the boys got off the train because they were going in another direction.
And three of us continued to Prague.
And we arrived there late at night.
And everything was closed.
And we went to the main railway station and spent the night sleeping on a table.
And the next day was my birthday, my 15th birthday.
It's how I recall the exact date.
In the morning, we found the, the refugee center or the center where they were trying to unify or bring together families.
And at that time, there was still just total confusion in Europe about finding people.
And the radio was constantly announcing that so-and-so has returned or so-and-so is looking for so-and-so, information about so-and-so.
And that is when I learned the date-- it was my birthday-- that my mother has not survived.
They had some Red Cross [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] files that apparently [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] carried her as, as, for, for dead.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These files may have been also compiled by people who came, who returned, who would tell them that so-and-so did not survive.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] The next step, again, was to be sent somewhere for delousing or for, for fumigation.
And we were-- a couple of us were sent to a rehabilitation [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] place, which was in the countryside.
And was a-- there were string of castles that were before the war owned by [INAUDIBLE], who owned a, a lot of the industry in Czechoslovakia.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] These castles were expropriated because they were of German origin.
And it was the first time that we slept on a regular bed.
I even had a nightgown, was embroidered name of count.
And we set the tables and used forks and knives, which was something that we haven't done for three years.
Well, these were all children, mostly orphans, who, who were just there in the country and played in gardens and, and ate food.
And there were volunteers, I guess, who took care of us and tried to bring us back home alive.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We started writing our memoirs there.
The drawing that I showed you, I actually drew for several of the boys.
And years later, they showed it to me.
We were together, starting our memoirs because we-- at that time, we wanted to somehow record what we still remembered.
Yes.
And it's amazing what memory does.
Because when I read what I wrote that year in 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] sometimes is reading the story of somebody else.
And it isn't me, that I cannot pin it down.
And after a while, your memory blurs, and you don't know whether you really remember something or whether you read it in your own diary.
Yes.
But those were all destroyed or taken away from me.
And this is why we felt that it's time to quickly record it.
At the time, I had grandiose plans for a detailed memoir.
But other priorities prevented me from doing it.
So I chose to simply write a synopsis, which I still have today.
I had a cousin who survived, who was from a mixed marriage, so she did not go to Auschwitz.
She was interned in a special camp, which was no picnic.
It was a horrible camp as well.
But she managed to survive.
She was much younger than my mother.
And there was a possibility there in my mind, go and live with her and her family.
But shortly afterwards, some former neighbors and a friend of my parents who survived the war wanted me to join her.

And after visiting my cousin and seeing how little we had in common and how little understanding they have, I accepted the offer of this other lady whose name is Vera Lowenback, to accept her hospitality.
And she actually had three survivors in her house.
And she herself was a survivor.
I obviously considered coming to the United States, but I was not enthusiastic to go to Israel.
And I don't remember that I was directly approached at that time by anybody.
